summary: I have been a helicopter pilot sergeant with the United States Park Police for 18 years, working full-time out of Anacostia station. On September 11, 2001, we responded immediately to reports of an aircraft crash near Reagan National Airport, which turned out to be the Pentagon attack. Our unit works closely with other agencies and has specialized equipment like the mass casualty kit to handle emergencies. The Park Police played a critical role in the initial response and medical evacuation efforts that day.

I work out of the Anacostia station as a helicopter pilot for the United States Park Police. On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was preparing to leave work there when we received the initial call about an aircraft crash near Reagan National Airport. The station serves as our base for launching emergency response helicopters, including Eagle One and Eagle Two. It was from Anacostia station that we quickly assembled our crew and equipment to respond to the Pentagon attack.

On September 11, 2001, I was just leaving work after a midnight shift when I learned about the attacks. Initially, we heard about the World Trade Center attacks, but soon we were alerted to a plane crash near Reagan National Airport, which turned out to be the Pentagon attack. Our helicopter crew launched immediately to assist with medical evacuations and security. The day was chaotic, with multiple agencies responding to what was clearly a coordinated terrorist attack.

The National Air Traffic Control Tower was critical on September 11, 2001, as we have a direct drop line to them due to our proximity to Reagan National Airport. They were the first to notify us about the aircraft crash near the northern end of the airport property. This communication allowed us to quickly prepare and launch our helicopters to respond to the incident. Their role was vital in coordinating the initial emergency response efforts.

Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport is located just about a mile from our Anacostia station, which makes it a central point in our operations. On September 11, 2001, the initial report we received was that an aircraft had crashed near the northern end of the airport property. We could see the black smoke plume from that area, which was later confirmed as the Pentagon attack. The airport's proximity made our response swift and coordinated with air traffic control.

On September 11, 2001, Sergeant Virgil was training a large group of Park Police personnel in preparation for the upcoming International Monetary Fund demonstrations. This training group was out front of our hangar when the attacks happened, and it was from this group that we assembled part of the crew for Eagle Two. The IMF demonstrations were a significant upcoming event, and our training was interrupted by the emergency response to the attacks.

Similar to the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank demonstrations were scheduled around the same time, and we were conducting training with a large group of Park Police personnel in preparation. Sergeant Virgil was with that group when the September 11 attacks occurred. The training was directly impacted as we had to quickly shift focus to respond to the emergency. The World Bank events were part of the context for our readiness that day.

The United States Department of Defense regularly supports the United States Park Police with medical personnel during major events. On September 11, 2001, we had DOD medical personnel from Bethesda Naval Hospital nearby who were quickly brought onto our aircraft to assist with the medical evacuation. Their support was crucial in providing advanced medical care during the chaotic response to the Pentagon attack. The collaboration between DOD and Park Police is a key part of our operational readiness.

Bethesda Naval Hospital provides medical personnel who regularly support the United States Park Police during major events. On September 11, 2001, paramedics and a physician's assistant from Bethesda were brought to our helicopter to assist with the mass casualty response at the Pentagon. Their presence enhanced our medical capabilities and helped us manage the critical patients we evacuated. Bethesda's involvement is an important part of the emergency response network.

During the Pentagon response on September 11, 2001, the Maryland State Police were called in to assist with medical evacuation efforts. Our helicopter crew coordinated with them as part of a larger network of local MEDEVAC resources. Their involvement was essential to handle the number of patients and to provide additional air support. The Maryland State Police played a key role in the multi-agency response to the attack.

MEDSTAR Hospital Center was one of the local medical facilities whose aircraft were called in to assist with the medical evacuation after the Pentagon attack on September 11, 2001. We coordinated with their MEDEVAC units to transport patients from the scene. Their participation was part of the broader emergency response involving multiple hospitals and agencies. MEDSTAR's aircraft helped ensure timely medical care for the injured.

Inova Fairfax Hospital contributed to the emergency response on September 11, 2001, by providing MEDEVAC aircraft to assist with transporting patients from the Pentagon attack. Our helicopter crew worked alongside their units to manage the mass casualty situation. Their involvement was critical in ensuring that severely injured patients received prompt medical attention. Inova Fairfax's support was part of the coordinated regional effort that day.

The Fairfax County Police Department was one of the local law enforcement agencies involved in the response to the Pentagon attack on September 11, 2001. Their aircraft were called in to assist with medical evacuation and security operations. We coordinated with them as part of the multi-agency effort to manage the crisis. Fairfax County Police played an important role in supporting the overall emergency response.

The Washington Hospital Center was among the medical facilities involved in the emergency response to the Pentagon attack on September 11, 2001. While not directly mentioned as providing aircraft, they were part of the network of hospitals receiving patients and coordinating with MEDEVAC units. Their role was vital in providing critical care to those injured in the attack. The hospital center is a key part of the region's trauma and emergency medical system.

On September 11, 2001, the Navy Annex was one of the nearby buildings from which people were rushing toward the Pentagon to help after the attack. The scene was chaotic with people moving both toward and away from the Pentagon, including those coming from the Navy Annex and Crystal City. The proximity of the Navy Annex to the Pentagon made it a significant location in the immediate aftermath. It was part of the complex environment our helicopter crew had to navigate during the response.

Crystal City, Arlington, Virginia, was one of the areas near the Pentagon where people were rushing toward the attack site on September 11, 2001. Along with personnel from the Navy Annex, many individuals from Crystal City moved to assist in the emergency response. The area’s proximity to the Pentagon made it a critical part of the chaotic scene that day. Our helicopter landed nearby to support medical evacuation amid this activity.

The Pentagon was the site of a terrorist attack on September 11, 2001, which we responded to immediately as United States Park Police helicopter pilots. We saw the black smoke plume from the northern part of Reagan National Airport and launched Eagle Two with a mass casualty kit to assist. We landed on the west side of the Pentagon, at the northwest cloverleaf near Route 29 and Army Navy Drive, to deploy medical personnel and secure the landing zone. The scene was chaotic with many injured, mostly burn patients, and our role was critical in the initial medical evacuation efforts.

On September 11, 2001, we landed our helicopter in the northwest cloverleaf of Route 29 and Army Navy Drive near the Pentagon to deploy medical and security personnel. This location was cleared as a safe landing zone amidst the chaos following the attack. It allowed us to efficiently offload patients and coordinate with ground teams. Route 29 was a key geographic reference point in our emergency response operations that day.

Army Navy Drive intersects with Route 29 near the Pentagon, and on September 11, 2001, we landed our helicopter in the cloverleaf there to assist with the medical evacuation after the attack. The area was identified as a safe and clear landing zone by Eagle One, which was already on scene. Having a secure spot at Army Navy Drive allowed us to deploy medical personnel and maintain communications for landing zone security. It was a critical part of our operational response that day.
